proposals.hypothesis: By developing mechanically robust, shaped pellets from a series of functionally distinct but fragile PCNs and arranging them in a designed multi-bed sequence, we can create a single, continuous hybrid process that synergistically combines their individual selectivities to achieve unprecedented separation performance for complex mixtures.
proposals.method: We will select a target ternary or quaternary gas mixture (e.g., involving C2F6, CO2, N2, CH4) based on industrial relevance. For each separation sub-task (e.g., bulk removal, impurity capture, final polishing), we will synthesize or obtain a known high-performance but fragile PCN (e.g., anion-pillared frameworks, hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks). We will apply and optimize pelletization techniques, such as extrusion-spheronization with judiciously selected polymeric binders, to produce robust spherical pellets while preserving porosity and adsorption capacity. The shaped pellets will be characterized for mechanical strength (crush testing), textural properties, and single-component/mixture adsorption (using IAST for prediction). A multi-column breakthrough setup will be used to experimentally validate the designed sequence, comparing performance to single-bed and powder-based systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable methodology for transforming lab-scale, powder-form PCNs into practical adsorbent forms suitable for process intensification. It will demonstrate a novel process design paradigm that moves beyond single-adsorbent systems, enabling the sequential application of multiple 'best-in-class' materials. The outcome will be a blueprint for achieving ultra-high purity or multi-product recovery from complex streams in a single, efficient unit operation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual materials but in the integrated system: the strategic combination of pelletization to enable the use of fragile, state-of-the-art PCNs in a designed multi-bed sequence creates a new, synergistic purification platform that is more than the sum of its parts, a concept not yet fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: In-situ Synchrotron Diffraction and Molecular Simulation Elucidating Guest-Induced Framework Dynamics for Enhanced Gas Separation
proposals.motivation: The exceptional separation performance of flexible or stimuli-responsive porous materials, such as the anion-pillared frameworks studied by Krishna, is often attributed to dynamic structural adaptations upon guest adsorption. However, a direct, real-time correlation between microscopic structural changes and macroscopic adsorption/selectivity data is lacking. Understanding this dynamic response is crucial for rationally designing the next generation of adaptive adsorbents with precise molecular recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high separation performance in selected ultramicroporous frameworks arises from specific, reversible, and guest-specific structural distortions (e.g., pillar tilting, linker rotation) that optimize host-guest electrostatic interactions, and that these dynamics can be captured and quantified in operando.
proposals.method: We will employ in-situ synchrotron-based powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) coupled with a custom gas dosing system to monitor the crystal structure of a model flexible MOF (e.g., SIFSIX-3-Zn or a related anion-pillared framework) during stepwise adsorption/desorption of pure components and binary mixtures (e.g., CO2/N2, C2H6/C2H4, C2F6/CHF3). The time-resolved diffraction patterns will be refined using the Rietveld method. Concurrently, we will perform molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the TraPPE force field, integrating the experimental lattice parameters as constraints to model the dynamic response and calculate the evolving Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-property relationship by linking transient framework geometries to separation metrics. It will provide a general methodology for characterizing framework flexibility and validate the use of flexible force fields in molecular simulations for predicting mixture adsorption in dynamic systems.
proposals.novelty: While in-situ diffraction of gas adsorption is known, its application to capture the *transient* structural states of a flexible framework during *competitive mixture* adsorption, combined with MD simulations to deconvolute the contributions of different guests to the dynamics, is a novel and powerful approach to understanding separation mechanisms.

proposals.title: Accelerated Discovery of Pore-Engineered MOFs via Microfluidic Combinatorial Synthesis and Optical Proxy Screening for High-Performance Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: The discovery of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with precisely tuned pore chemistry and geometry for challenging gas separations (e.g., CO2 capture, fluorocarbon purification) remains slow and labor-intensive. Traditional synthesis and subsequent characterization via high-pressure gas adsorption isotherms create a severe bottleneck, limiting the exploration of vast chemical design spaces. A paradigm shift towards high-throughput, proxy-based screening is urgently needed to accelerate the identification of optimal linkers for targeted separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid, optical proxy measurements (e.g., fluorescence quenching, indicator displacement, or spectral shifts upon gas exposure) performed on microfluidically synthesized MOF particle libraries will strongly correlate with their equilibrium adsorption selectivity and capacity, enabling the efficient prediction of high-performance materials.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular microfluidic platform for the continuous, combinatorial synthesis of MOF particles by varying linker identity and ratio from a library of functionalized dicarboxylates and nitrogen-donor ligands. The synthesized particles will be deposited in an addressable array format. A parallelized optical screening station will expose array spots to target gases (e.g., CO2, C2F6, N2) and measure changes in fluorescence intensity or UV-Vis absorption spectra. Machine learning (specifically, a random forest regression model) will be trained on this optical data, using a subset of materials validated by traditional volumetric adsorption and IAST calculations, to predict adsorption performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, integrated workflow that drastically reduces the time from MOF concept to performance prediction, moving from months to days. It will produce a validated, data-driven model linking rapid optical responses to adsorption thermodynamics, generating specific candidate MOFs for benchmark separations. The methodology will provide a generalizable platform for the accelerated discovery of porous materials.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput MOF synthesis exists, coupling it directly to optical proxy screening—an approach borrowed from organic electronics—to predict gas adsorption performance is a novel and non-obvious strategy. This bypasses the slow adsorption isotherm measurement, creating a fundamentally new discovery pipeline for adsorption-based separations.

proposals.title: Integrated High-Throughput Discovery and Shaping of Metal-Organic Frameworks for De-Risked Scale-Up in Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: The discovery of novel adsorbents for challenging gas separations, such as CO2 capture or fluorocarbon purification, is often bottlenecked by the unpredictable performance loss during shaping (e.g., pelletization) and scale-up. Pre-2026, high-throughput (HT) synthesis and screening are well-established for powder materials, but their results frequently fail to translate to industrially viable shaped forms due to binder interactions, pore blockage, and mechanical stress. This disconnect significantly delays and de-rails the commercialization of promising materials identified in the lab.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating HT synthesis and gas adsorption screening directly with a continuous pellet production line, we hypothesize that structure-performance relationships can be established which explicitly account for shaping effects, enabling the predictive discovery of adsorbent formulations that retain excellent separation performance (validated by IAST) in their final, scalable form.
proposals.method: We will employ a robotic platform for the parallel solvothermal synthesis of a library of ultramicroporous MOFs and HOFs, focusing on ligand variants to tune electrostatic interactions (inspired by Krishna's prior work). Powder X-ray diffraction and automated volumetric gas adsorption (N2, CO2, C2F6) will screen for promising candidates. Critically, promising powders will be immediately processed using a twin-screw extruder and spheronizer into mm-sized pellets with a standardized polymeric binder. These pellets will undergo rapid column breakthrough experiments with relevant gas mixtures (e.g., CO2/N2 or C2F6/air), with performance metrics (e.g., working capacity, selectivity) fed back into the discovery loop to guide subsequent synthesis iterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, integrated pipeline that accelerates the translation of adsorbents from discovery to kg-scale production of performance-validated pellets. It will generate a unique dataset correlating molecular structure, shaping parameters, and shaped-adsorbent separation performance, providing critical guidelines for de-risking industrial scale-up. The outcome will be a demonstrated, scalable adsorbent pellet with benchmark separation performance for a targeted gas mixture.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop feedback between high-throughput molecular discovery and continuous shaping manufacturing, treating the shaped form as the primary unit of analysis from the outset rather than as a post-discovery engineering step. This proactive 'scale-up by design' approach is distinct from the sequential, linear methodology dominant in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Fabrication of Ultrathin, Defect-Free Membranes from Electrostatic Potential-Matched Networks for High-Performance Gas Separations
proposals.motivation: The development of continuous, energy-efficient membrane processes for gas separations like CO2 capture and fluorocarbon purification is a critical industrial challenge. While advanced adsorbents like anion-pillared frameworks and hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs) show excellent selectivity in batch adsorption, their translation into high-flux, scalable membranes remains limited. Pre-2026 work demonstrates the power of electrostatic potential matching for achieving benchmark separation performance in powders, but this principle has not been leveraged in a membrane format.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fabricating ultrathin, oriented membranes from specifically tuned networks (e.g., anion-pillared frameworks or stable HOFs), we can translate their exceptional equilibrium adsorption selectivity, validated by Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST), into unprecedented mixed-gas membrane selectivity and flux, overcoming the ubiquitous permeability-selectivity trade-off.
proposals.method: We will synthesize continuous, sub-500 nm thick membranes using a combination of in-situ growth and secondary seeded growth on porous alumina or polymeric supports. Key frameworks will include the recently reported electrostatic potential-matched anion-pillared framework for C2F6 purification and the stable HOF from Krishna's work. Membrane quality will be assessed by scanning electron microscopy and single-gas permeation to detect defects. Separation performance will be evaluated for target mixtures (e.g., CO2/N2, C2F6/C2H6/CH4) using a custom-built Wicke-Kallenbach cell, with results analyzed against IAST-predicted selectivities from pure-component adsorption isotherms measured on powder samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct pathway to transform high-performance powder adsorbents into practical membranes, providing a critical missing link between adsorption science and process engineering. It will deliver the first reported mixed-gas separation data for membranes based on these novel electrostatic-tuned networks, setting new benchmarks for fluorocarbon purification and CO2 separation. The study will also elucidate the relationship between framework microstructure in thin films and the preservation of intrinsic adsorption selectivity in permeation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the pre-2026 concept of electrostatic potential matching—proven for adsorption—to the design and fabrication of ultrathin membranes, a step beyond conventional material screening. This moves from evaluating powders to creating a continuous, oriented separation device that leverages the same precise host-guest interactions for a different, scalable operational mode.

proposals.title: Bio-Inspired Chemical Gating in MOFs for Ethylene Purification: Designing a Selectivity Filter Against Ethylene via Electrostatic Potential Matching
proposals.motivation: The production of polymer-grade ethylene requires the removal of trace C2 impurities like ethane and acetylene, an energy-intensive process. Current adsorbents often co-adsorb ethylene, reducing selectivity and efficiency. A material that actively excludes ethylene while binding impurities would represent a transformative advance for the petrochemical industry, enabling more sustainable separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely tailoring the electrostatic potential and chemical functionality of an ultramicroporous framework's pore walls—creating a bio-inspired 'selectivity filter'—we can engineer strong, specific binding sites for ethane and acetylene while generating a repulsive or neutral chemical environment for ethylene, leading to its effective exclusion.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations to screen and design linker molecules and metal nodes that create pore environments with optimal quadrupole moment matching for acetylene and enhanced van der Waals contacts for ethane. Promising candidates, particularly anion-pillared or hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs), will be synthesized. Adsorption isotherms for pure components and binary/ternary mixtures (C2H4/C2H6/C2H2) will be measured, and selectivity will be predicted and validated using the Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST). Breakthrough column experiments will assess dynamic separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel design principle for molecular sieves based on active chemical rejection rather than passive size exclusion. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how pore electrostatics govern selectivity in C2 hydrocarbon separations and yield a prototype adsorbent with benchmark purification performance for ethylene.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly translating the biophysical concept of an ion channel selectivity filter to gas separation, aiming not to adsorb the primary target (ethylene) but to chemically repel it, which is a counter-intuitive strategy compared to conventional adsorption-based purification.

proposals.title: Hydrogen-Bond Tuning in Robust HOFs for the Kinetic Separation of C4 Hydrocarbon Isomers
proposals.motivation: The separation of C3/C4 hydrocarbon isomers, such as n-butane/isobutane or 1-butene/isobutene, is critical for producing high-octane fuels and polymer precursors, yet remains energy-intensive via cryogenic distillation. Pre-2026, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have shown promise for these separations, but their stability under industrial conditions can be limited. Hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs) offer exceptional chemical tunability and potential stability but have been underexplored for separating these larger, more complex molecules compared to small gas separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically designing and positioning hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor sites within the pores of a robust HOF, we can create precise electrostatic potential landscapes that differentially interact with the dipole and quadrupole moments of C4 isomers, enabling high-selectivity kinetic or equilibrium-based separation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular HOFs based on a scalable, stable tecton, such as a tetrahedral tetracarboxylic acid, incorporating secondary functional groups (e.g., -NH2, -OH, -CF3) to modulate pore chemistry and hydrogen-bond strength. High-throughput computational screening using Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will guide the selection of top candidates by predicting adsorption isotherms. The Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) will be applied to predict mixture selectivity. Experimentally, we will measure single-component and binary mixture adsorption isotherms for C4 isomers (n-butane, isobutane, 1-butene, isobutene) and assess separation performance via breakthrough column experiments under realistic conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for using hydrogen-bond control in HOFs for the separation of molecules beyond small gases, specifically targeting the challenging C4 hydrocarbon market. It will yield a novel, stable HOF material with benchmark separation performance for a key industrial mixture, bridging the gap between fundamental pore chemistry design and applied separation science.
proposals.novelty: While HOFs have been explored for gas purification (e.g., hexafluoroethane), applying hydrogen-bond electrostatic matching to the kinetic and equilibrium separation of larger, flexible C4 isomers is a novel and non-obvious extension of the concept, moving from rigid, small molecules to a more complex fluid-phase separation challenge.

proposals.title: Sequential versus Simultaneous Adsorption: A Process-Oriented Evaluation of C2H6 and C3H8 Removal from Natural Gas Using Tailored Microporous Frameworks
proposals.motivation: The purification of natural gas (CH4) from heavier alkanes (C2H6, C3H8) is energy-intensive. Pre-2026 research, including our own, focuses on developing adsorbents with high selectivity for simultaneous C2H6/C3H8 removal over CH4. However, the process-level efficiency of this simultaneous approach versus a staged, sequential removal strategy has not been rigorously challenged or optimized, representing a significant gap in translating material performance to industrial practice.
proposals.hypothesis: A staged separation process, utilizing two distinct adsorbents each optimized for the selective removal of either C2H6 or C3H8 from a CH4-rich stream, will yield a lower overall energy penalty and higher process efficiency compared to a single-step process targeting simultaneous co-adsorption of both heavier alkanes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined experimental and modeling approach. First, we will synthesize and characterize two anion-pillared or ultramicroporous MOFs/HOFs, tuning pore chemistry (e.g., via electrostatic potential matching) for high C2H6/CH4 selectivity in one, and high C3H8/CH4 selectivity in the other. Single and multi-component adsorption isotherms will be measured and fitted using the Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST). Second, we will perform dynamic breakthrough simulations (using the Transport-Dispersed Bi-Band model, TDBB) for both a single-bed simultaneous process and a two-bed sequential process. Process performance metrics (energy duty, productivity, CH4 recovery) will be calculated and compared using a validated process simulator.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a critical, process-centric framework for evaluating adsorbent performance beyond pure selectivity and capacity metrics. It will deliver a quantitative comparison of separation strategies, identifying the conditions (feed composition, pressure) where sequential removal is advantageous. The findings will guide the targeted design of next-generation adsorbents for specific roles in a separation train.
proposals.novelty: While material development for C2+ hydrocarbon separation is mature, the explicit challenge to the paradigm of simultaneous removal and the systematic design of a complementary adsorbent pair for a staged process is a novel, systems-engineering approach to adsorption-based purification.

proposals.title: Engineering Ultramicroporous Metal–Organic Frameworks for Kinetic Exclusion of C2H6 and C3H8 in Ethylene Purification
proposals.motivation: The separation of ethylene (C2H4) from ethane (C2H6) and propane (C3H8) is a critical, energy-intensive industrial process. Pre-2026 research, including our own, has predominantly focused on equilibrium-based separations using thermodynamic selectivity, often via IAST modeling. However, achieving high selectivity under high-pressure, mixed-gas conditions remains challenging. A paradigm shift towards kinetic control could enable superior performance in realistic process conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely tuning the pore aperture and internal surface chemistry of ultramicroporous MOFs, we can achieve a dominant kinetic exclusion mechanism for C2H6 and C3H8 over C2H4, where the diffusion rates of the larger/less polarizable alkanes are severely hindered while ethylene adsorbs rapidly.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular, anion-pillared ultramicroporous MOFs (e.g., SIFSIX or TIFSIX variants) with systematic variations in pore size (via ligand extension) and electrostatic potential (via anion substitution). We will employ time-dependent breakthrough (TDBB) experiments with ternary C2H4/C2H6/C3H8 mixtures to directly measure kinetic selectivity and separation performance under dynamic flow conditions. Pure-component adsorption isotherms and IAST will be used to benchmark equilibrium selectivity, while pulsed-field gradient NMR and frequency response techniques will quantify diffusional time constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clear design framework for kinetically controlled hydrocarbon separations in MOFs, moving beyond pure equilibrium-based screening. It will deliver a prototype material with benchmark kinetic selectivity for C2H4 purification, validated by scalable column breakthrough experiments. The findings will challenge the primacy of IAST in predicting performance for such strongly diffusion-limited systems.
proposals.novelty: While kinetic separation is known for small gas pairs (e.g., O2/N2), its deliberate exploitation for C2/C3 hydrocarbon separations in tailored ultramicroporous MOFs is largely unexplored. This approach intentionally subordinates high equilibrium capacity to achieve superior dynamic selectivity, a non-intuitive strategy given the field's focus on adsorption enthalpy and uptake.

proposals.title: Inverse Adsorption Strategy: Electrostatic Potential Matching in an Anion-Pillared Framework for the Selective Recovery of C2H6 and C3H8 from Natural Gas
proposals.motivation: Natural gas upgrading requires the removal of CO2 and N2, but the co-adsorption of valuable C2+ hydrocarbons (ethane, propane) on conventional materials leads to significant product loss and energy-intensive recovery. A process that selectively retains these heavier hydrocarbons for direct, high-purity recovery would transform natural gas processing into a more efficient and profitable operation. This addresses a critical gap in pre-2026 adsorption-based separation technologies, which primarily focus on removing light gases.
proposals.hypothesis: The principle of electrostatic potential (ESP) matching, previously demonstrated for the selective capture of hexafluoroethane (C2F6) over N2 and CH4, can be inverted. We hypothesize that by fine-tuning the ESP within an ultramicroporous, anion-pillared metal-organic framework, we can create a stronger affinity for the higher quadrupole moments and polarizabilities of C2H6 and C3H8 over the dominant methane component.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize the stable anion-pillared framework (e.g., SIFSIX or TIFSIX variants) known for its tunable electrostatic environment. Single-component and multicomponent (CH4/C2H6/C3H8/CO2/N2) adsorption isotherms will be measured experimentally. The experimental data will be analyzed using the Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) to predict mixture selectivity and guide material refinement. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will be employed to calculate the electrostatic potential landscapes within the pores and correlate them with the binding energies of different gas molecules, enabling a rational, iterative design cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel 'inverse' separation paradigm for natural gas, shifting the target from impurity removal to valuable product recovery. It will deliver a material and a validated process concept for the energy-efficient, one-step concentration of C2+ hydrocarbons, potentially setting a new benchmark for hydrocarbon recovery selectivity. The findings will expand the application scope of ESP-matching principles beyond fluorocarbon capture.
proposals.novelty: While Krishna's prior work used ESP matching to adsorb the *heaviest* component (C2F6) from a light mixture, this proposal inverts the target to selectively adsorb *intermediate* hydrocarbons (C2H6, C3H8) from a mixture where methane is the lightest and most abundant component. This is a non-obvious reversal of the separation objective using the same fundamental principle.

proposals.title: Electrostatic Tuning of an Anion-Pillared Ultramicroporous Framework for High-Performance Binary C2H2/C2H4 Separation
proposals.motivation: The separation of acetylene (C2H2) from ethylene (C2H4) is a critical and energy-intensive step in the petrochemical industry to produce polymer-grade C2H4. Pre-2026, physisorptive separation using metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offers a promising energy-efficient alternative to cryogenic distillation, but achieving simultaneously high C2H2 uptake and exceptional C2H2/C2H4 selectivity under ambient conditions remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electrostatic potential distribution within an anion-pillared ultramicroporous framework, previously optimized for fluorocarbon separations, can be systematically reconfigured via targeted ligand functionalization to create a pore environment that exhibits benchmark binary C22H2/C2H4 separation performance through enhanced host-guest electrostatic interactions with C2H2.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular anion-pillared frameworks (e.g., SIFSIX or TIFSIX variants) with systematically varied organic ligands to fine-tune pore size and electrostatic potential. High-pressure single-component adsorption isotherms for C2H2 and C2H4 will be measured at 298K. The Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) will be applied to predict binary mixture selectivity from pure-component isotherm fits. The most promising candidate will undergo dynamic breakthrough (TDBB) experiments with 1:99 C2H2/C2H4 mixtures to validate its separation performance and stability under realistic flow conditions. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will elucidate the binding sites and interaction energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, structurally tunable MOF platform with quantified, benchmark separation metrics (uptake and selectivity) for the binary C2H2/C2H4 challenge. It will establish a clear structure-property relationship between ligand-induced electrostatic tuning and separation performance, providing a design strategy for other challenging hydrocarbon separations.
proposals.novelty: While anion-pillared frameworks are known for strong electrostatics, their deliberate reconfiguration for the binary C2H2/C2H4 separation—diverting from their established use for CO2 or fluorocarbon capture—is a novel application. The inversion of the design goal from complex ternary mixtures to a high-purity binary separation targets a different, industrially paramount endpoint.

proposals.title: Design of an Anion-Pillared Ultramicroporous MOF for Kinetic Separation of Ethylene/Ethane Mixtures
proposals.motivation: The separation of ethylene (C2H4) from ethane (C2H6) is one of the most energy-intensive industrial processes, primarily relying on cryogenic distillation. Adsorptive separation using Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) offers a promising alternative, but most research focuses on equilibrium-based selectivity, often targeting subtle thermodynamic differences. Kinetic separation, exploiting differences in diffusion rates, could offer superior performance for challenging mixtures with similar adsorption affinities, yet it remains underexplored for C2 hydrocarbons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely tuning the pore aperture and electrostatic environment of an anion-pillared, ultramicroporous MOF, we can create a size-exclusion-like regime where the slightly larger kinetic diameter of ethane is exploited to achieve selective, rapid diffusion of ethylene, thereby prioritizing kinetic over equilibrium control.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational screening approach, building on the successful design principles of SIFSIX and TIFSIX anion-pillared frameworks. Using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations, we will first model the equilibrium adsorption (IAST) of C2H4/C2H6 in candidate structures. Subsequently, we will use Transition State Theory (TST) methods, specifically the TST-based diffusion barrier (TDBB) algorithm, to calculate the kinetic diffusion barriers for each component. The target MOF architecture will feature a pillared-layer structure where the pillar (e.g., a modified pyrazine or a custom organic linker) and the metal node (e.g., Cu, Zn) are chosen to create a rigid pore with an aperture between 3.6-4.2 Å, strategically positioned to interact more strongly with the π-electron cloud of ethylene via electrostatic potential matching, thereby lowering its diffusion barrier relative to ethane.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, rationally designed MOF candidate optimized for kinetic C2H4/C2H6 separation, validated by high-throughput computational screening. It will establish a clear design protocol for kinetic separations in ultramicroporous materials, moving beyond pure equilibrium-based design. The proposed material is expected to demonstrate benchmark kinetic selectivity and high ethylene productivity in breakthrough simulations.
proposals.novelty: While Krishna's prior work excelled in equilibrium separation via electrostatic tuning (e.g., for CO2 and C2F6), this proposal pivots to deliberately design a framework where equilibrium selectivity is secondary to kinetic selectivity for C2 hydrocarbons, a non-obvious target given their similar sizes and polarizabilities. The integration of TST-based kinetic screening with electrostatic potential matching for diffusion barrier modulation represents a novel design paradigm.

proposals.title: Pore Geometry Engineering in Anion-Pillared MOFs for the Selective Purification of Ethane or Acetylene from Ternary Mixtures
proposals.motivation: The separation of C2 hydrocarbons (ethylene/ethane/acetylene) is a critical yet energy-intensive industrial process, typically relying on cryogenic distillation. Adsorptive separation using metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) offers a promising energy-efficient alternative. While prior work, including from our group, has focused on CO2 or fluorocarbon separations, the targeted recovery of ethane or acetylene as the primary product from complex ternary mixtures remains a significant challenge with major implications for petrochemical and polymer industries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely tuning the pore geometry and electrostatic environment within anion-pillared ultramicroporous frameworks, we can achieve a thermodynamic reversal of adsorption affinity, enabling the selective binding and purification of either ethane (C2H6) or acetylene (C2H2) over ethylene (C2H4) from ternary gas mixtures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular anion-pillared MOFs (e.g., SIFSIX or TIFSIX variants) with systematically varied pore dimensions through ligand extension and anion substitution. High-pressure single-component and mixture (C2H2/C2H4/C2H6) adsorption isotherms will be measured experimentally. The Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) will be applied to predict mixture selectivity from pure-component isotherms. Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will be employed to elucidate the binding sites and host-guest interactions, correlating pore geometry with separation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for pore geometry control to target specific C2 hydrocarbon products, moving beyond ethylene-centric separations. It will deliver one or more benchmark adsorbents with demonstrated high selectivity and working capacity for C2H6 or C2H2 purification. The study will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between molecular shape, quadrupole moment, and framework electrostatics in governing mixture adsorption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in strategically re-engineering frameworks, previously optimized for linear molecules like CO2, to exploit the subtle size and shape differences among C2 hydrocarbons for the direct purification of ethane or acetylene, a non-obvious target given the historical focus on ethylene purity.

proposals.title: Leveraging Electrostatic Potential Matching in a Cd-MOF for the Trace Removal of Perfluorinated and Hydrofluorocarbon Impurities from Industrial Gas Streams
proposals.motivation: The purification of high-value industrial gases, such as NF3 for semiconductor manufacturing or refrigerants like R-134a, is critically hampered by the presence of trace, size-similar perfluorinated (PFC) and hydrofluorocarbon (HFC) impurities. Pre-2026, achieving the required ultra-high purity is energy-intensive, relying on cryogenic distillation. Adsorptive separation using Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) offers a promising alternative, but few materials exhibit the necessary combination of high selectivity and capacity for these challenging, weakly interacting gas pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Cd-MOF, previously shown to enable benchmark C2F6 purification via electrostatic potential (ESP) matching, possesses a tunable, ultramicroporous environment that can be reconfigured to achieve high selectivity for other critical trace separations involving molecules with similar sizes but distinct quadrupole moments and polarizabilities.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the reported Cd-MOF and characterize it via PXRD and N2 porosimetry. High-pressure, single-component adsorption isotherms will be measured for target gas pairs (e.g., NF3/CF4, R-134a/R-125) at 298 K. The experimental data will be fitted using the Dual-site Langmuir model. The Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) will be applied to predict mixture selectivities from the pure-component isotherms. These predictions will be validated through dynamic breakthrough (TDBB) experiments with ternary gas mixtures under relevant industrial conditions (trace impurity, ~1000 ppm). Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations will be performed to elucidate host-guest interactions and confirm the role of ESP matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship for ESP-matching MOFs in trace impurity removal, moving beyond a single case study. It will provide a validated, efficient screening protocol (IAST + TDBB) for identifying MOFs for challenging industrial separations and deliver experimental performance data for at least two new, high-impact gas pairs, advancing the rational design of adsorbents for energy-efficient gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While the Cd-MOF's performance for C2F6 is known, its systematic application to other critical, size-similar PFC/HFC pairs is unexplored. The novelty lies in explicitly testing the generality of the ESP-matching principle across a family of industrially relevant molecules and providing a full experimental and theoretical validation of its separation mechanism.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Driven Screening of Organic Linkers for High-Performance Adsorptive Separation of Fluorocarbon Mixtures
proposals.motivation: The purification of high-value fluorocarbons like hexafluoroethane (C2F6) from complex mixtures is critical for the semiconductor and electronics industries. While recent work, including that from our group, has demonstrated the efficacy of anion-pillared and hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs) for such separations, the vast chemical space of potential organic linkers remains largely unexplored. A systematic, high-throughput approach is needed to identify linkers that optimize the delicate balance of adsorption capacity and selectivity, particularly for challenging ternary mixtures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models trained on existing gas adsorption isotherm data for porous frameworks can accurately predict the separation performance of novel, synthetically accessible organic linkers, thereby identifying candidates that surpass the performance of current benchmark materials for C2F6 purification.
proposals.method: We will construct a database of gas (e.g., C2F6, CF4, SF6, N2) adsorption isotherms and structural descriptors (e.g., pore size, electrostatic potential distribution, functional groups) from published literature and our group's prior work. Using this data, we will train supervised learning models, including gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost) and graph neural networks (GNNs), to predict key performance metrics like adsorption uptake and Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) selectivity. The trained models will then screen a large in-silico library of organic linker structures to rank candidates, which will be validated through molecular simulations and targeted synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a predictive computational tool for the rational design of framework materials for fluorocarbon separation, accelerating the discovery process. It will also yield one or more novel, ML-identified linker-based frameworks with experimentally validated, benchmark separation performance for industrially relevant ternary mixtures.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to MOF screening for gas storage, its targeted use for predicting linker efficacy in the specific context of fluorocarbon mixture separation—where electrostatic interactions and pore chemistry are paramount—is novel. This approach directly bridges high-throughput computational screening with the specific separation expertise and experimental validation capabilities of our research group.

proposals.title: Electrostatic Interaction-Engineered MOF Thin-Film Membranes for High-Performance Propylene/Propane Separation
proposals.motivation: The separation of propylene from propane via cryogenic distillation is an energy-intensive industrial process. Adsorptive separation using ultramicroporous MOFs with tailored electrostatic potentials, as demonstrated by Krishna's group for CO2 and fluorocarbon separations, offers a promising alternative. Translating these high-selectivity adsorbents into continuous, scalable membrane formats remains a critical challenge for practical implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: That the high selectivity for specific olefins achieved in powder adsorbents via electrostatic potential matching can be preserved and exploited in a continuous, defect-free MOF thin-film membrane, leading to a simultaneous enhancement in propylene permeability and propylene/propane selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize the anion-pillared ultramicroporous MOF (e.g., SIFSIX or TIFSIX variants) as a thin film on a porous alumina support using a scalable, contra-diffusion method. Film morphology and thickness will be controlled via precursor concentration and reaction time. The separation performance will be evaluated using a custom-built Wicke-Kallenbach cell for single and mixed-gas (C3H6/C3H8 50:50) permeation tests at ambient temperature. Experimental adsorption isotherms for the pure components will be measured and used as input for the Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) to predict mixture selectivity, which will be compared with the actual membrane performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct pathway from high-performance adsorbent powders to functional membranes, providing a crucial proof-of-concept for energy-efficient olefin/paraffin separation. It will yield a novel membrane material with benchmark propylene/propane separation performance and deliver fundamental insights into the relationship between intrinsic adsorption selectivity and membrane transport selectivity in electrostatically tuned ultramicroporous frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While Krishna's group has excelled in designing adsorbents with exceptional selectivity via electrostatic interactions, applying this precise design principle to fabricate high-performance thin-film membranes for olefin purification is unexplored. The novelty lies in the integrated materials design and processing strategy to translate a powder's adsorption affinity into a membrane's permeation selectivity.

proposals.title: Scalable Synthesis and Fixed-Bed Column Validation of HOF-TDBB for Industrial-Scale Hexafluoroethane Purification
proposals.motivation: The purification of hexafluoroethane (C2F6) from complex mixtures containing nitrogen and tetrafluoromethane (CF4) is a critical industrial challenge, particularly for the semiconductor and electronics industries. While the hydrogen-bonded organic framework HOF-TDBB has demonstrated record-setting selectivity for C2F6 in laboratory-scale adsorption experiments, its transition to practical application is hindered by the lack of a scalable synthesis protocol and validation under dynamic, continuous flow conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reproducible, kilogram-scale synthesis of HOF-TDBB can be developed, and that the material will maintain its exceptional C2F6/CF4/N2 selectivity and capacity when packed into a fixed-bed adsorption column, enabling efficient separation of the ternary mixture in a continuous process.
proposals.method: We will first develop a scalable solvothermal synthesis protocol for HOF-TDBB, optimizing solvent composition, reactant concentration, and crystallization conditions to produce multi-gram batches with consistent porosity and purity, as verified by PXRD and N2 adsorption. The adsorbent will be pelletized with a suitable binder, characterized, and packed into a laboratory-scale stainless steel column. Breakthrough experiments will be conducted with simulated ternary gas mixtures (C2F6/CF4/N2) at varying feed compositions, pressures, and flow rates. Performance will be evaluated via breakthrough time, dynamic working capacity, and product purity, with results modeled using the Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) and dynamic column breakthrough simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first reported protocol for the scalable production of a stable HOF for gas separation and deliver critical engineering data on its dynamic separation performance. It will bridge the gap between the material's exceptional intrinsic properties and its potential for industrial implementation, offering a clear pathway for the use of HOFs in energy-efficient gas purification processes.
proposals.novelty: While HOF-TDBB's static adsorption performance is known, its behavior in a continuous flow, fixed-bed system is unexplored. The development of a scalable synthesis for this specific, high-performing HOF and its subsequent validation in a realistic separation process represents a significant advancement beyond proof-of-concept studies.

proposals.title: Linker-Functionalized Hydrogen-Bonded Organic Frameworks for the High-Performance Separation of Xe/Kr and C2 Hydrocarbon Mixtures
proposals.motivation: The separation of noble gases (Xe/Kr) and light hydrocarbons (e.g., C2H2/C2H4, C2H4/C2H6) is critical for industrial gas production and the petrochemical industry, yet remains energy-intensive. Pre-2026 work has demonstrated the promise of porous materials like Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) and Hydrogen-Bonded Organic Frameworks (HOFs) for these separations, but achieving a balance of high selectivity, stability, and scalability remains a challenge. HOFs, with their facile synthesis and potential for precise pore functionalization, offer a promising but underexplored platform for these gas separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic modification of the organic linkers in a robust HOF scaffold—by introducing specific functional groups (e.g., -F, -CF3, -CN, -CH3)—will allow fine-tuning of the pore electrostatic environment and van der Waals interactions, thereby significantly enhancing adsorption selectivity for target gas pairs without compromising framework stability.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of isoreticular HOFs based on a proven, stable tetrahedral building block (e.g., tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)pyrazine or analogous). Linker functionalization will be achieved via pre-synthetic modification, introducing targeted substituents at the phenyl rings. The materials will be characterized by PXRD, BET surface area analysis, and FT-IR. Single- and multi-component gas adsorption isotherms (for Xe, Kr, C2H2, C2H4, C2H6) will be measured up to 1 bar at 298 K. Experimental data will be analyzed using Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) to predict mixture selectivities, and results will be correlated with Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) simulations to elucidate host-guest interaction mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship between HOF linker chemistry and separation performance for industrially relevant gas pairs, providing a design blueprint for next-generation adsorbents. It will deliver one or more HOF materials with benchmark combination of selectivity and capacity, validated by breakthrough experiments, advancing HOFs from promising materials to practical candidates for gas purification.
proposals.novelty: While linker functionalization is common in MOFs, its systematic application to fine-tune the pore chemistry of robust, scalable HOFs for high-performance gas separations is a novel and non-obvious strategy, moving beyond the typical focus on MOFs and leveraging the unique, dynamic nature of HOFs for selective recognition.

proposals.title: Fabrication of Ultrathin, Oriented Hydrogen-Bonded Organic Framework (HOF) Membranes for High-Performance, Energy-Efficient CO2 Separation
proposals.motivation: Membrane-based gas separation offers a low-energy alternative to thermally-driven processes like distillation, crucial for carbon capture and industrial gas purification. However, achieving simultaneously high permeability and selectivity in scalable, defect-free thin films remains a key challenge. Hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs) show exceptional molecular recognition and stability, as demonstrated in recent powder-based adsorption studies, but their potential as continuous, oriented membranes is largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fabricating continuous, oriented, and ultrathin (<500 nm) films of a stable, electrostatically tuned HOF (e.g., HOF-FJU-1 or analogous structures), we can create membranes that translate the high adsorption selectivity observed in powder beds into superior, industrially relevant mixed-gas separation performance, particularly for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixtures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a stable HOF, selected for its proven separation performance (e.g., from recent work on C2F6 purification), as a colloidal suspension of nanocrystals. Ultrathin films will be fabricated via a combination of spin-coating and Langmuir-Blodgett techniques on porous polymeric supports, aiming for sub-micron thickness and controlled crystal orientation. Membrane performance will be evaluated using a custom-built Wicke-Kallenbach permeation cell for single and binary gas mixtures (CO2, N2, CH4), with selectivity compared to predictions from Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST) applied to pure-component adsorption isotherms measured via volumetric adsorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for fabricating continuous, oriented HOF membranes, directly linking their adsorptive selectivity to permeation performance. It will provide the first experimental data on the mixed-gas separation efficiency of HOF thin films, validating their potential for scalable, energy-efficient separation processes and creating a new design paradigm for molecular-sieving membranes.
proposals.novelty: While HOFs have been studied as adsorbents, their processing into oriented, ultrathin continuous membranes is a novel and non-trivial step. This proposal uniquely combines the emerging concept of electrostatic potential matching in HOFs—a pre-2026 design strategy for selectivity—with advanced thin-film fabrication to create a new class of molecular-sieving membranes.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Robotic Synthesis and Screening of SIFSIX-TEPE Analogs for the Efficient Separation of Industrially Relevant Fluorocarbon Mixtures
proposals.motivation: The separation of fluorocarbons (F-gases) like hexafluoroethane (R-116) from complex mixtures is a critical industrial challenge for environmental protection and refrigerant purification. Pre-2026 work has shown that anion-pillared SIFSIX materials, such as SIFSIX-TEPE, achieve benchmark performance via electrostatic potential matching, but their synthesis and screening for diverse gas pairs is slow and empirical. An automated approach is needed to systematically explore the vast chemical space of organic linkers to discover materials with superior selectivity and capacity for challenging separations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic variation of the organic linker in the SIFSIX-TEPE framework, guided by high-throughput robotic synthesis and testing, will reveal structure-property relationships that enable the rational design of materials with optimized pore chemistry and geometry for the separation of specific ternary and binary fluorocarbon mixtures.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput robotic platform for parallel solvothermal synthesis of SIFSIX-TEPE analogs, where the TEPE (tetra(4-pyridyl)ethylene) linker is substituted with a diverse library of nitrogen-donor linkers of varying length, flexibility, and functionalization. Automated powder X-ray diffraction will confirm phase purity. A parallelized, high-pressure volumetric gas adsorption analyzer will then screen single-component isotherms for key gases (e.g., C2F6, C2H2F4, CF4, SF6) at 298K. IAST calculations will be performed automatically on the collected data to predict mixture selectivities.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rapid discovery pipeline for next-generation SIFSIX adsorbents, generating a unique dataset linking linker structure to adsorption performance for fluorocarbons. It will yield one or more new materials with experimentally validated, benchmark separation performance for a targeted industrial mixture, moving beyond single exemplary frameworks to a family of tunable materials.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput methods have been applied to MOFs, their focused application to the tunable and highly performant SIFSIX platform for fluorocarbon separation is novel. This approach moves beyond serendipitous discovery to a targeted, data-driven exploration of linker space within a proven topology.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Cationic Pore Polarization Handles for Predictive Adsorption Tuning
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the systematic cation replacement strategy from Idea A with the automated synthesis and rapid screening pipeline from Idea B. We will construct a combinatorial library of cationic functional groups (e.g., varying size, polarity, hydrogen-bonding capability) and use high-throughput robotic synthesis to produce a large array of metal-organic framework (MOF) variants. Each variant will be rapidly characterized using automated, low-volume gas adsorption measurements for key probe gases (e.g., CO2, N2, H2O, CH4) to quantify pore polarity and affinity. The resulting high-dimensional dataset will be analyzed with machine learning models to decode the structure-function relationship, ultimately generating a predictive design map for tailoring pore surface chemistry to achieve precise separation selectivities.

ideas.title: Adapting High-Contaminant Tolerance Strategies from Catalytic Converter Design to C2H6/C2H4 Separation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from automotive catalysis, where catalysts are rigorously tested under increasingly harsh, contaminant-rich conditions to evaluate real-world viability. The analogous solution involves systematic feed contamination studies to find performance limits and guide material engineering for tolerance. Adapted to the HOF-NBDA context, the research will evaluate adsorbent performance with challenging, higher C2H6/C2H4 feed ratios (e.g., 5/95 to 25/75 v/v) beyond the initial 1/99 test. The goal is to map the separation performance degradation curve, identify the dominant mechanism of failure under high ethane load, and use these insights to propose targeted modifications to the HOF structure or synthesis to improve its selectivity and capacity resilience for more industrially relevant feedstocks.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Discovery and Direct Scale-Up of Shaped Adsorbents
ideas.description: This research integrates high-throughput automated synthesis and rapid gas adsorption screening with a continuous, scalable pellet production line. The synergy lies in using the high-throughput platform not just to discover promising ligand variants for pore tuning, but to immediately test their performance when processed into the final shaped adsorbent form (e.g., pellets or monoliths) under realistic manufacturing conditions. This approach identifies and eliminates formulations that perform well as powders but fail upon shaping or scaling, a critical bottleneck. Consequently, the research accelerates the translation of novel adsorbents from discovery to kilogram-scale production of performance-validated pellets, de-risking scale-up by design.

ideas.title: Operational Stability of Ultrathin, Tunable Membranes Under Continuous, Cycled Gas Separation
ideas.description: This research will fabricate ultrathin membranes from tuned porous networks and subject them to prolonged, continuous operation with repeated adsorption-desorption cycles under realistic temperature, pressure, and feed gas conditions. The core investigation will be to correlate microscopic structural changes—such as framework collapse, pore blockage, or interfacial delamination—with the evolution of macroscopic separation performance (selectivity and permeability) over time. The goal is to establish design principles for materials and membrane architectures that prioritize long-term stability and easy regenerability, moving beyond single-point performance metrics to develop membranes truly viable for industrial gas separation applications.

ideas.title: Operando Structural Dynamics in Industrial Gas Separation
ideas.description: This research direction aims to perform in-situ, time-resolved structural analysis (e.g., via synchrotron X-ray scattering or advanced spectroscopy) on a separation material while it is exposed to complex, wet, and trace-contaminated industrial feed gases. The core objective is to observe and quantify how the material's pore network dynamically deforms, swells, or reconfigures in real-time in response to the competitive adsorption of target molecules, water vapor, and trace contaminants. By simultaneously measuring the separation performance (e.g., selectivity, permeability) under these identical, realistic conditions, the study will directly link specific structural transients to changes in separation efficiency. This approach reveals the fundamental mechanisms of performance decay, hysteresis, or unexpected stability, guiding the design of next-generation materials with engineered structural resilience for industrial applications.

ideas.sourceTitle: Hydrogen bond unlocking-driven pore structure control for shifting multi-component gas separation function

ideas.title: Computationally Guided Direct Synthesis of Kinetically Stable HOFs for Ethane Capture
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput computational screening—leveraging DFT and machine learning models trained on catalyst and materials databases—to predict novel organic linker molecules and their assembly conditions. The computational goal is specifically to identify linkers that, when combined with hexafluorosilicate or similar anions, will directly crystallize into a kinetically stable, neutral HOF with the optimal pore chemistry for C2H6 selectivity, eliminating the thermal transformation step. The predicted top candidates are then synthesized under the computed conditions, validating the direct synthesis pathway. This creates a virtuous cycle where experimental results refine the computational models for the targeted discovery of directly synthesizable, transformation-free HOFs.

ideas.title: Causative Design of Pore Polarity via Integrated Cation Screening, Synthesis, and Direct Quantification
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach moves beyond simple cation substitution by first using DFT calculations to screen candidate cations within a specific, stable metal-organic framework to predict their impact on electrostatic potential and polarizability. A targeted subset is then synthesized, prioritizing isoreticular frameworks to minimize structural variance. The effective pore polarity is directly and quantitatively measured using techniques like in-situ infrared spectroscopy of probe molecules and frequency-dependent dielectric spectroscopy, explicitly accounting for cation mobility and framework interactions. This creates a closed-loop, causative design principle for tuning pore environment polarity with predictive power.

ideas.title: Design HOFs with synergistic sites for other gas mixtures

ideas.title: High-Throughput Combinatorial Screening of MOF Linkers via Microfluidic Synthesis and Optical Property Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts high-throughput screening methodologies from organic electronics to the discovery of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with tuned pores. In organic electronics, microfluidic combinatorial synthesis is used to create libraries of conjugated polymers, which are then rapidly screened via optical and electronic proxy measurements (e.g., absorption spectra, energy levels) that predict final device performance, bypassing slow full-device testing. Adapted to MOF research, a microfluidic synthesis platform would generate a vast array of MOF variants using automated linker mixing. The resulting materials would then be screened not with slow gas adsorption, but with rapid, parallelized proxy assays—such as fluorescence-based indicator displacement or optical adsorption changes in the presence of target gases—that correlate with pore size, chemistry, and adsorption capacity. This workflow would dramatically accelerate the identification of optimal ligands for precise pore engineering.

ideas.title: Molecular Sieve Design via Bio-Inspired Gating: Excluding Ethylene by Mimicking Ion Channel Selectivity
ideas.description: This idea expands the original inverse problem of designing pores to exclude ethylene by drawing an analogy from biophysics. The analogous problem is how biological ion channels selectively filter one ion over another of similar size. The solution involves a chemically tuned 'selectivity filter' that matches the target ion's coordination preferences. Adapted to porous materials design, the research idea is to computationally model and synthesize frameworks where the pore's interior surface chemistry is precisely tailored. The goal is to create a chemical environment that provides strong, specific binding sites for C2 impurities like ethane and acetylene through optimal van der Waals, dipole, or quadrupole interactions, while leaving ethylene with no favorable interaction points, effectively causing it to be excluded from adsorption. This moves beyond pure size exclusion to active chemical rejection.

ideas.title: High-throughput virtual screening of metal-organic frameworks for ethane-selective adsorption using computational drug discovery pipelines
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established high-throughput virtual screening methodology from computational drug discovery to the problem of discovering metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for ethane-selective adsorption. In the analogous field, pipelines screen vast digital libraries of small molecules by simulating their docking into a protein's binding site, using a combination of physics-based scoring (force fields) and machine learning models trained on known actives to predict binding affinity and selectivity. The adapted solution applies this pipeline logic to MOF discovery: a vast in-silico library of MOF structures is generated combinatorially. For each MOF, a simulation analogous to molecular docking—Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) or DFT calculations for key candidates—computes the adsorption of C2H6 and C2H4. A machine learning surrogate model, trained on a subset of these calculations using MOF structural features as descriptors, rapidly predicts adsorption selectivity and capacity for the entire library. This filters millions of potential MOFs down to a shortlist of top-performing, synthetically accessible candidates for subsequent experimental synthesis and testing, dramatically accelerating the discovery of materials for ethane/ethylene separation.

ideas.title: Inert Host, Reactive Guest: Bio-Inspired Impurity Sequestration for Ethylene Purification
ideas.description: This idea inverts the target from ethylene purification to capture by designing materials where pore tuning selectively captures impurities, letting ethylene pass through unadsorbed. The analogy is drawn from enzymology, where enzymes often have active sites designed to bind and modify specific cofactors or inhibitors while ignoring the primary substrate. The analogous solution is the precise engineering of a binding pocket for a trace molecule within an otherwise inert structure. Adapted to materials science, this involves creating a porous host framework that is intrinsically non-adsorptive to ethylene. Within this host, specific 'molecular traps' are installed—localized functional groups or coordinatively unsaturated metal sites with ultra-high affinity and selectivity for key impurities like acetylene. The material's overall architecture ensures high permeability for ethylene, while the strategically placed traps sequester contaminants, achieving purification by removing the unwanted components rather than binding the desired product.

ideas.title: Sequential Multi-Bed Purification Systems Using Shaped, Mechanically Robust Porous Coordination Network Pellets
ideas.description: This research direction develops a hybrid purification process by first applying spherical pellet shaping techniques to a series of fragile, functionally distinct porous coordination networks (PCNs), such as MOFs or COFs, to grant them the mechanical integrity for packed-bed use. These robust, shaped pellets of different network materials are then arranged in a designed multi-bed or layered column sequence, where each stage is tuned for a specific separation task (e.g., pre-concentration, selective removal of a key impurity, or final polishing). This enables a single, continuous process to achieve ultra-high purity or recover multiple high-value products from complex mixtures, overcoming the limitations of single-material adsorbents. The core synergy is enabling the practical, sequential application of advanced but previously too-fragile networks in an integrated system for unprecedented separation performance.

ideas.title: Design HOFs with Non-Planar, Protein-Mimetic Pockets for C2H6 Trapping
ideas.description: This idea applies the principle of biomimetic molecular recognition from structural biology to the challenge of C2H6/C2H4 separation. In enzymes, selectivity arises from complex, three-dimensional binding pockets, not flat surfaces. The analogous solution involves designing protein structures with cavities complementary to a specific substrate. Adapted to HOFs, this means deliberately moving away from two-dimensional, sheet-like structures to engineer frameworks with intricate, non-planar topologies. These topologies should create well-defined, confined pockets where the walls are functionalized to provide optimal van der Waals contact and weak electrostatic interactions with C2H6. The shape and chemical environment of the pocket would be a poor fit for C2H4, leading to high selectivity based on molecular recognition rather than simple size-sieving on a flat plane.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Spectral Signal Demixing for Complex Off-Gas Separation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a quaternary mixture represents the most complex case for designing separations of refinery or chemical plant off-gases. By drawing an analogy from signal processing and analytical spectroscopy—where techniques like multivariate curve resolution separate complex, overlapping signals from many sources without a pre-defined limit—we adapt this solution to chemical separation design. The adapted research idea proposes to treat multi-component off-gas streams not as simple mixtures of 3-4 key species, but as complex systems requiring 'demixing.' This involves developing a process synthesis framework that uses multiple, orthogonal physical or chemical properties (akin to spectral dimensions) to design separation sequences—potentially involving hybrid membrane-adsorption-distillation systems or advanced cyclic processes—that can be systematically targeted to resolve five or more valuable or problematic components from these off-gas streams, thereby increasing recovery and efficiency beyond conventional designs.

A combinatorial array of solvent polarity, proton donor/acceptor additives, and molecular concentration is screened to identify parameters that favor the thermodynamic sink of the neutral framework from the outset. In-situ analytical techniques, primarily powder X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy, provide immediate feedback on phase formation, allowing for the dynamic adjustment of synthesis conditions to bypass the need for a post-synthetic thermal transformation step.

ideas.title: Direct Shaping of Neutral HOFs via Catalytic Pelletization Techniques
ideas.description: This research direction combines the goal of directly synthesizing the neutral, nonpolar HOF-NBDA framework with advanced catalyst pelletizing and binder techniques. The synergy lies in applying shaping and mechanical reinforcement methods—such as extrusion, spray-drying, or granulation with tailored polymeric binders—to the synthesis mixture *before* or *during* framework formation. This enables the direct production of shaped monoliths, pellets, or beads of the target neutral HOF, eliminating the need for post-synthetic thermal transformation of a charged precursor. The result is a scalable route to mechanically stable, directly usable forms of neutral HOFs for applications like hydrocarbon separation or storage, where both framework neutrality and physical robustness are critical.

ideas.title: Dual-Phase CO2 Capture via Hemoglobin-Inspired Cooperative Binding
ideas.description: This research adapts the cooperative binding solution from biochemistry (hemoglobin's oxygen transport) to the problem of scaling carbon capture. The core idea is to develop a CO2-affinity enhanced material whose binding behavior is not static but dynamic and condition-responsive. Analogous to hemoglobin loading O2 in the lungs and unloading it in tissues, the material would be engineered for a dual-phase function: first, a moderate-affinity 'capture phase' optimized for the high-volume, lower-concentration environment of flue gas, where initial binding triggers a structural change; second, a high-affinity 'retention phase' activated by that initial binding, which strongly holds the CO2 during subsequent handling or lower-pressure processing steps. This mimics the biological efficiency of a single molecule operating in two distinct environments, aiming to create a capture material that is both highly efficient in initial uptake and robust against CO2 loss before sequestration.

ideas.title: Thermally Integrated, Low-Energy Regeneration System for Flue Gas Carbon Capture
ideas.description: This redesigned system focuses on minimizing the regeneration energy penalty of CO2-affinity materials by engineering them for low-temperature desorption cycles (e.g., using moisture or mild vacuum swings) and physically integrating the capture unit with an adjacent industrial heat source. The capture process uses a material optimized for fast kinetics at flue gas temperatures, while the regeneration is driven by waste heat (e.g., <100°C) from a co-located facility, such as a low-grade steam loop or cooling water system. This thermal integration creates a continuous, low-operational-cost cycle that maintains high capture efficiency while ensuring the net carbon reduction and economic metrics are positive for industrial deployment.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Self-Healing Adsorbents via Dynamic Hydrogen Bond Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational inverse design directly with long-term stability testing to engineer porous materials with dynamic, self-correcting hydrogen bond networks. Machine learning models will be trained on high-throughput cycle stability data to inversely predict ligand modifications that introduce sacrificial or reversible bonds within the pore, enabling structural recovery during desorption phases. The synthesized materials will then undergo accelerated lifecycle testing, with the resulting degradation and regeneration data fed back to refine the computational models. This creates a synergistic loop where computational design targets regeneration mechanisms, and experimental stability data validates and teaches the model, ultimately enabling the design of adsorbents with programmable, extended operational lifespans.

ideas.title: Apply Topology Optimization from Photonics to Design Hydrogen Bond Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse design methodology of topology optimization, widely used in photonics to design complex metamaterials and waveguides, to the problem of engineering hydrogen bond networks in porous materials like MOFs or COFs. In the analogous photonics problem, a target optical function is achieved by algorithmically modifying material distribution. Here, we translate this to specify a target chemical function (e.g., selective guest binding, specific ion transport path, catalytic activity) for a material's pores. A computational inverse design engine would then work within the synthetic constraints of a chosen framework topology to output the optimal 3D arrangement and chemical identity of hydrogen-bonding functional groups on the organic ligands to achieve that target, generating non-obvious, high-performance designs for synthesis.

ideas.title: Operando spectroscopic control of dynamic CO2 capture materials in flue gas
ideas.description: This research direction develops a closed-loop system where operando Raman or IR spectroscopy is used not just to observe, but to actively control a CO2-affinity enhanced material during flue gas carbon capture. The spectrometer provides real-time feedback on the state of key hydrogen bonds involved in CO2 adsorption and material regeneration. This data is used to dynamically adjust process conditions—such as temperature or gas flow—to optimize capture capacity in response to changes in flue gas composition or to trigger regeneration cycles precisely before performance degrades, moving from scheduled maintenance to condition-based operation.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Bioassay Protocols for Industrial Gas Separation Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous, multi-tiered validation framework from pharmaceutical bioassay development to the testing of gas separation materials. In pharma, drug candidates are validated using complex biological matrices like blood plasma through protocols involving spike-and-recovery, specificity panels, and robustness testing. Adapted here, a standardized testing protocol would be developed for separation materials using real industrial feed gases. The core method involves executing a sequence of controlled experiments where the target gas is spiked into authentic, multi-component industrial streams. The protocol would systematically introduce and vary challenging conditions—such as fluctuating concentrations of water vapor, trace contaminants (VOCs, H2S, siloxanes), and particulates, alongside realistic pressure and temperature swings—to rigorously quantify the material's separation performance (selectivity, capacity, kinetics) and, crucially, map its functional boundaries and identify specific failure modes in a complex, non-ideal environment that mirrors actual industrial operation.

ideas.title: Adapting Catalyst Deactivation Studies to Predict Adsorbent Longevity
ideas.description: This research adapts the established framework for studying long-term catalyst deactivation from the field of heterogeneous catalysis to the problem of adsorbent stability. In catalysis, durability is not merely tested but understood by identifying specific deactivation mechanisms (sintering, coking, poisoning) via accelerated aging, post-mortem characterization, and modeling. The adapted idea applies this methodology to adsorption materials, aiming to systematically identify analogous adsorbent degradation mechanisms—like pore collapse, active site poisoning by trace contaminants, or irreversible structural changes—under process-relevant cycling. By employing accelerated lifetime tests combined with advanced characterization (e.g., in-situ XRD, porosimetry, spectroscopy) after set cycle intervals, the research will link specific material changes to performance decay. This mechanistic understanding will then inform the development of predictive models for long-term stability and the design of targeted, intelligent regeneration protocols to restore capacity, transforming durability assessment from an empirical observation to a predictable, engineerable material property.

ideas.title: Operando Spectroscopic Control of Dynamic Bonding in Ultrathin Separation Membranes
ideas.description: This research direction involves fabricating ultrathin, tunable network membranes for gas separation and integrating them directly into an operando spectroscopy cell. The core innovation is using real-time Raman or IR spectroscopy to monitor the state of dynamic bonds (e.g., hydrogen bonds) within the membrane material as gas mixtures flow through it. This live spectroscopic data is then used to actively control external stimuli (like humidity, temperature, or applied voltage) to modulate the bond states, thereby dynamically optimizing the membrane's separation performance for specific gas pairs. This approach moves beyond passive observation or static optimization, enabling adaptive, smart membranes whose selectivity can be tuned on-demand based on direct spectroscopic feedback from the working material.

ideas.title: Adapting Patch-Clamp Microfluidics for Single-Crystal Adsorption Kinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of the patch-clamp technique from cellular electrophysiology to the study of adsorption kinetics. In patch-clamping, a micropipette creates a sealed environment around a tiny patch of cell membrane to study single ion channels. Analogously, we propose designing a microfluidic platform with a specialized microcavity that can form a hermetic seal against a specific facet of a single crystal, creating an isolated adsorption 'patch'. This sealed micro-reactor would allow for the precise delivery of gas stimuli and the direct, high-fidelity measurement of gas uptake and surface diffusion kinetics on that individual crystalline plane, providing fundamental, facet-resolved insight into adsorption mechanisms free from bulk or polycrystalline averaging effects.

ideas.title: Adapting Corrosion Monitoring to Track Hydrogen Bond Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from electrochemical corrosion science, where in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) dynamically monitors the formation and breakdown of protective interfacial bonds. The analogous problem of observing transient bond states under operational conditions is solved here by transferring the core methodology to a different spectroscopic domain. We propose using in-situ, time-resolved vibrational sum-frequency generation (VSFG) spectroscopy to monitor hydrogen bond network dynamics at the solid-gas interface of porous adsorbents during gas uptake. This surface-specific technique, operable under realistic synthesis or adsorption conditions, would track the evolution of hydrogen bonding signatures in real-time, revealing the kinetic pathways and intermediate states involved in the bond unlocking that governs gas capture and release.

ideas.title: Apply Photoswitchable Molecular Motor Concepts to HOFs for Dynamic Polarity Control
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the field of molecular machines and nanotechnology, where light-driven rotary molecular motors (like overcrowded alkenes) are used for precise, stepwise conformational and polarity changes. The analogous solution is to use specific light wavelengths to trigger unidirectional rotation, altering the system's properties in a controlled cycle. This solution is adapted to HOFs by designing a framework that incorporates such a photoswitchable molecular motor as a key structural component. Upon irradiation, the motor's rotational states will dynamically and reversibly switch the pore's polarity between distinct levels (e.g., nonpolar, moderately polar, highly polar), enabling advanced, stimuli-responsive adsorption profiles for gases like CO2, CH4, or H2O based on their differing dipole moments.
