ideas.title: Mechanistic Investigation of Degradation Pathways During Cyclic Process Conditions via Integrated Operando Characterization
ideas.description: This study moves beyond monitoring performance loss to actively diagnose the root causes of material degradation during adsorption-desorption cycling. By integrating operando spectroscopic, diffraction, and gas analysis tools into the cycling reactor, we correlate real-time structural and chemical changes with performance metrics. This enables the identification of specific failure mechanisms—such as active site poisoning, phase collapse, or support interaction breakdown—under realistic process conditions. The mechanistic understanding gained directly informs the design of more durable materials and optimized regeneration protocols.

ideas.title: Dual-Mode Self-Correcting Scaffolds for Fatigue-Resistant Dynamic Pores
ideas.description: This research designs materials where dynamic pore adjustment is driven by the reversible breaking of primary coordination bonds under an external stimulus (e.g., light, redox potential). To overcome the fatal flaw of irreversible misalignment, a secondary, templating network of hydrogen bonds is integrated. These weaker bonds temporarily break during the primary transformation but their inherent directionality and pre-programmed interaction sites guide the precise reformation of the primary network, ensuring the pore shape returns to its original geometry. This dual-mode, self-correcting mechanism provides robust, fatigue-resistant cyclability without cumulative structural drift.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Screening and Adaptive Processing for Shaping Diverse Fragile Porous Networks
ideas.description: This project establishes a closed-loop research system to systematically identify viable shaping pathways for fragile porous coordination networks, rather than applying a single method by default. It employs automated parallel synthesis to produce a library of chemically diverse networks, which are then subjected to a matrix of shaping processes under varied conditions. The key innovation is the immediate, automated characterization of the shaped bodies for critical metrics like crystallinity, porosity, mechanical strength, and adsorption capacity. Machine learning analyzes this dataset to predict the optimal shaping method and parameters for a given network's structural features, enabling the rational design of robust shaped bodies tailored to specific applications like gas storage or catalysis.

ideas.title: Reconstructing Real-Time Pore Network Dynamics via Multi-Modal Sensing and Physics-Informed Machine Learning
ideas.description: This research overcomes the limitations of direct imaging by developing a system that infers real-time, nanoscale pore structure dynamics from a suite of high-frequency, in-situ indirect measurements. An experimental core simultaneously collects ultrasonic, nanomechanical, and transient thermal response data from a sample undergoing gas adsorption/desorption cycles. A physics-informed neural network, trained on a vast dataset from coupled molecular-dynamics and continuum simulations, inverts these multi-modal signals to reconstruct a dynamic 3D model of the evolving pore network and fluid distribution. This approach provides unprecedented temporal resolution and operates under realistic environmental conditions, enabling the study of previously inaccessible rapid and complex pore-gas interactions.

ideas.title: Mechanically-optimized architected materials via inverse design from catalytic pore principles
ideas.description: This research develops a new class of high-strength, lightweight architected materials by using the vast library of pore and microstructure architectures from catalyst engineering as an inspiration source, but rigorously re-engineering them for mechanical performance. A computational materials design pipeline inversely screens catalytic pore geometries (e.g., hierarchical, zeolitic, mesoporous) using finite element analysis to identify those with exceptional predicted strength-to-weight and toughness properties. These selected architectures are then fabricated using high-performance structural materials (e.g., cross-linked polymers, metal alloys, or ceramics) via precision additive manufacturing or templated synthesis techniques. The outcome is a data-driven methodology to generate novel, robust structural metamaterials whose design principles are borrowed—but not copied—from the functionally distinct domain of heterogeneous catalysis.

ideas.title: Integrated microfluidic-interferometric platform for picogram-resolved adsorption kinetics on single crystals
ideas.description: This system combines a precisely engineered microfluidic gas delivery platform with embedded, on-chip optical interferometry to measure adsorption kinetics on individual microcrystals. The crystal is mounted within a monolithic micro-optical cavity, where changes in adsorbed mass are read out as shifts in the cavity's resonant frequency, providing direct mass uptake data independent of flow or pressure transients. This design isolates the crystal's intrinsic kinetic response from systemic noise, enabling the study of low-capacity or weakly adsorbing systems under dynamic conditions. The approach yields fundamental insights into surface heterogeneity and diffusion barriers with unprecedented sensitivity for single-crystal studies.

ideas.title: Adapting Semiconductor Layer Transfer for Ultrathin Membrane Fabrication
ideas.description: This idea adapts the semiconductor industry's 'Smart Cut' layer transfer technique to solve the fabrication challenge for ultrathin, continuous gas separation membranes. In the analogous field, this method reliably produces single-crystal silicon films on insulators by using ion implantation to define a cleave plane and a handle wafer for support. The adapted research idea involves synthesizing a bulk film of the tuned network material (e.g., a metal-organic framework or polymer of intrinsic microporosity) on a temporary substrate. A precise dose of ions is implanted to create a weakened layer at the target membrane thickness. A porous, robust support is then bonded to the film surface. Finally, controlled stress (thermal or mechanical) is applied to cleave the ultrathin membrane layer and transfer it onto the support, resulting in a continuous, defect-free, and scalable separation membrane with minimal thickness for maximum flux and energy efficiency.

ideas.title: Adapting Bio-imaging Techniques for In-Situ Tomography of Pore Dynamics During Gas Cycling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the time-resolved, rapid-freeze imaging techniques from structural biology (specifically cryo-EM) to study the dynamic response of pore structure to gas presence. The analogous solution involves initiating a dynamic process (gas adsorption/desorption), arresting it at precise moments, and capturing high-resolution 3D snapshots to reconstruct a temporal sequence. The adapted research idea is to develop an experimental setup where a porous material undergoes gas cycling within a specialized micro-reactor. At defined pressure or temporal points during the cycle, the system is rapidly quenched (e.g., via cryogenic or chemical fixation) to preserve the instantaneous pore structure. The sample is then imaged using in-situ electron tomography or X-ray micro-computed tomography. By repeating this cycle-quench-image protocol over multiple identical samples or cycles, a 4D dataset (3D structure over time) of the pore network's dynamic deformation, swelling, or collapse during gas interaction can be reconstructed, revealing previously inaccessible mechanistic details of adsorption-induced structural changes.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach isolates the specific failure mechanisms of separation materials by testing against precisely controlled and documented gas mixtures, rather than variable real-world feeds. It establishes a causative link between individual contaminant species and observed performance losses, such as selectivity decline or permeability loss. The protocol generates fundamental, reproducible data that defines the material's operational envelope and poisoning thresholds. This enables predictive modeling of material lifetime and informed selection for specific industrial applications based on known feed compositions.

ideas.title: Cascade Signal Processing for Multi-Bed Purification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single, perfectly selective purification step is necessary. Drawing an analogy from signal processing, where a cascade of tuned filters (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) sequentially removes specific noise components to isolate a clean signal, we propose a multi-bed or hybrid purification process. Instead of one material handling all impurities, a sequence of tuned materials or unit operations is designed. Each stage is optimized to remove a dominant, specific class of contaminant (e.g., one bed for aggregates, another for host cell proteins, a third for DNA), thereby 'conditioning' the stream for the next. This approach relaxes the extreme performance demands on any single material, can improve overall recovery by avoiding harsh single-step conditions, and may lead to more robust and cost-effective purification trains for biologics or fine chemicals.

ideas.description: This approach integrates operando Raman or IR spectroscopy with simultaneous atomic force microscopy (AFM)-based nanomechanical mapping within a single environmental cell. The system directly correlates spectroscopic signatures of hydrogen bond evolution with quantitative, spatially resolved measurements of local stiffness, adhesion, and energy dissipation during synthesis or gas adsorption. This dual measurement unambiguously distinguishes true bond unlocking from other spectral changes by linking them to mechanical property transitions, providing direct access to the energy landscape. The result is a robust, quantitative methodology that maps both the chemical state and the mechanical consequences of bond activation in real time.

This adaptive approach compensates for feed variability and material performance drift, preventing failure and optimizing resource use. The result is a robust, self-correcting purification process that reliably achieves high purity without the fragility of a fixed-sequence design.

ideas.title: Adapting Soil Biocementation for Shaped Network Reinforcement
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geotechnical engineering—Microbially Induced Calcite Precipitation (MICP)—to the problem of improving the mechanical strength of shaped porous networks. In MICP, bacteria are used to precipitate a mineral binder (calcite) within soil, binding particles and increasing strength while preserving pore space for fluid flow. Adapted to catalyst or material engineering, this involves infiltrating a shaped porous network (e.g., a catalyst pellet or structural scaffold) with a controlled bacterial culture and chemical feedstocks. The subsequent in-situ biomineralization deposits a reinforcing nanocrystalline phase at particle contacts and along pore walls, acting as a bio-derived binder. This process could create a strong, interlocked composite structure, improving crush strength and durability more uniformly than some traditional pelletizing techniques, while offering fine control over binder distribution to maintain critical pore architecture for mass transfer or other functions.

ideas.title: Investigate Material Fatigue and Self-Healing Cycles in Adsorbents Using Biomimetic Approaches
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from biomedical engineering, specifically the study of long-term stability and regeneration in artificial tissues and self-healing biomaterials, to the problem of adsorbent material degradation. The analogous problem involves analyzing mechanical fatigue and integrating repair mechanisms to sustain function over repeated use cycles. The adapted idea is to study the adsorbent's 'material fatigue'—tracking micro-crack formation, pore collapse, and active site poisoning over extended cycling—and to explore biomimetic, self-healing strategies. This could involve designing composite adsorbents with encapsulated healing agents (e.g., mobile precursors) or stimuli-responsive components that repair structural defects upon a specific trigger (e.g., temperature swing during desorption), thereby regenerating adsorption capacity and extending the material's practical lifespan.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Granulation Techniques for Shaping Fragile Coordination Network Powders
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of shaping fragile porous coordination network (PCN) powders into practical pellets by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical formulation science. In drug manufacturing, difficult-to-handle fine powders are transformed into robust granules via wet granulation using a liquid binder. Adapting this solution, the research would involve systematically applying a compatible solvent or polymer-binder solution to PCN powders to form agglomerates, followed by controlled drying. The goal is to develop a generalized granulation protocol that yields mechanically stable, spherical PCN pellets with preserved porosity and functionality, enabling their practical use in adsorption columns, catalysts, or sensors.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Continuous Manufacturing for Kilogram-Scale Pellet Production
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Pharmaceutical Continuous Manufacturing to the challenge of scaling adsorbent pellet production. The analogous field solved its scaling problem by moving from batch to integrated, modular continuous processing with real-time monitoring. The adapted research idea is to develop a continuous, scalable synthesis route by designing an interconnected platform that performs all steps—from precursor handling to final pellet shaping—in a controlled flow. This system will incorporate in-line process analytical technology (PAT) for real-time quality control, enabling the consistent, high-throughput production of kilogram quantities of the shaped adsorbent material with defined properties.

ideas.title: Cyber-Physical Synthesis with Inline PAT and Parallel Validation for Robust Kilogram-Scale Pellet Production
ideas.description: This project develops a robust, scalable synthesis system that integrates continuous production with real-time quality assurance to prevent off-specification manufacturing. A primary continuous reactor line is equipped with inline spectroscopic and porosimetry probes for real-time monitoring of critical adsorbent properties. This data feeds a closed-loop control system that automatically adjusts synthesis parameters to maintain product specifications. A secondary, small-scale parallel reactor runs accelerated stress tests and explores process extremes, providing continuous data to refine the control algorithms and ensure the main production line remains resilient to feedstock variability and operational drift.

ideas.title: Mechanistic investigation of failure pathways and predictive durability limits under accelerated multi-stress cycling
ideas.description: This research systematically forces and diagnoses material failure to move beyond measuring gradual capacity fade. It employs accelerated lifetime testing protocols that combine thermal, chemical, and mechanical stresses to rapidly reveal latent failure modes like fracture, poisoning, or phase segregation. In-situ and operando diagnostics are coupled with cycling to detect early-warning signatures of irreversible degradation. The outcome is a mechanistic failure map and predictive models for durability limits, enabling the design of more robust materials and defining safe operational envelopes for practical applications.
